title: How I learned to wait in line in Tokyo
summary: A small reflection on the patience that public spaces in Japan ask of you.
topic: How I learned to wait in line in Tokyo
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences (150–250 words) about a small social rule in another country that surprised you and changed your behaviour. Use past simple, present simple, and 'used to' for contrast.
hintStarters: Before I moved to Tokyo, I used to think queues were…
On my first day I stood at a bus stop where…
Now, even back home, I notice that I…
sampleText: Before I moved to Tokyo for my job, I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion. In my home city, people often pushed forward, used their elbows, and got on the bus first if they could. On my first morning in Tokyo, I stood at a busy platform and noticed something strange. Two long lines of people were waiting silently on the floor, exactly where small arrows were painted. Nobody talked, nobody looked at their phone too loudly, and nobody tried to move forward. When the train arrived, the people inside got off first, and only then did the two lines move into the carriages without any pushing. I felt a little embarrassed because I had walked straight to the door without looking at the arrows. After that day, I started to pay attention. I learned that the queue is more than a rule in Tokyo; it is a small daily act of respect for the strangers around you. Now, three years later, I queue carefully even when I visit my home city. Sometimes my friends laugh at me, but I do not mind. The habit costs me only thirty seconds, and it gives me a calmer morning every day.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Used to' for past habits or beliefs
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Used to + V' diễn tả niềm tin hoặc thói quen quá khứ đã thay đổi: 'I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion.' Bây giờ tôi nghĩ khác.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Used to + verb' shows a past belief or habit that has changed: 'I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion.' Now I think differently.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to give earlier context
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Had + V3' đặt một việc trước sự kiện chính trong quá khứ: 'I felt embarrassed because I had walked straight to the door without looking.' Hành động đi thẳng xảy ra trước cảm giác ngại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Had + past participle' marks something that happened before the main past event: 'I felt embarrassed because I had walked straight to the door without looking.' The walking happened before the embarrassment.

sampleTranslationVi: Trước khi tôi chuyển đến Tokyo vì công việc, tôi từng nghĩ xếp hàng chỉ là một gợi ý lịch sự. Ở thành phố quê tôi, người ta thường chen lên phía trước, dùng khuỷu tay, và lên xe buýt trước nếu có thể. Vào buổi sáng đầu tiên ở Tokyo, tôi đứng ở một sân ga đông đúc và để ý thấy điều gì đó lạ. Hai hàng dài người đang đợi im lặng trên sàn, đúng chỗ những mũi tên nhỏ được sơn. Không ai nói chuyện, không ai mở điện thoại quá to, và không ai cố chen lên trước. Khi tàu đến, những người bên trong xuống trước, và chỉ sau đó hai hàng mới di chuyển vào toa tàu mà không có sự chen lấn nào. Tôi cảm thấy hơi ngại vì tôi đã đi thẳng đến cửa mà không nhìn các mũi tên. Sau ngày đó, tôi bắt đầu chú ý. Tôi học được rằng xếp hàng không chỉ là một quy tắc ở Tokyo; nó là một hành động tôn trọng nhỏ hằng ngày dành cho những người lạ xung quanh bạn. Bây giờ, ba năm sau, tôi xếp hàng cẩn thận ngay cả khi tôi về thăm thành phố quê. Đôi khi bạn bè tôi cười tôi, nhưng tôi không bận tâm. Thói quen này chỉ tốn tôi ba mươi giây, và nó cho tôi một buổi sáng yên tĩnh hơn mỗi ngày.

options: I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion.

mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' for past belief.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence with 'even when'.
options: I queue carefully even when I visit my home city.
I queue carefully even though when I visit my home city.
I queue carefully when even I visit my home city.
I queue carefully even I visit my home city.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even when + clause' is the natural pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct meaning of 'respect' here.
options: consideration for other people
money for a ticket
a polite greeting word
a quiet song

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct verb form: 'The people inside ___ first.'
options: got off
get off
have got off
are getting off
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple 'got off' in a past narrative.

options: The habit costs me only thirty seconds.
The habit cost me only thirty second.
The habit costs to me only thirty seconds.
The habit is costing me only thirty seconds.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'costs' for a general truth; plural 'seconds'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct meaning of 'I do not mind'.
options: I don't get upset about it
I forget about it
I forbid it
I cannot do it
mcQuestions.explanation: 'I don't mind' = it doesn't bother me.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural opening contrast.
options: Before I moved to Tokyo for my job, I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion.
Before I moved to Tokyo for my job, I am using to think queues were just a polite suggestion.
Before I move to Tokyo for my job, I used to thinking queues were just a polite suggestion.
Before I had moved to Tokyo for my job, I used to think queues were just a polite suggestion.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple 'moved' + 'used to' + base verb in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple + used to + base verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do small public rules (like queueing) say something deeper about how a society sees strangers?
